ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 covers scallop food product broadly; no specific grilled scallop term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers scallops as a category; no grilled-preparation facet applied. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 refers to scallop as a substance; no grilled preparation code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of grilled scallops as served at a Chinese seafood restaurant in Singapore is approximately 6 pieces, weighing around 180 g total.

nutrient_source.title: Scallops, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried — adapted to grilled preparation; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 15175 (raw scallops) and Item 15176 (cooked scallops) used as primary reference
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked scallops (NDB 15176, 'Mollusks, scallop, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried') was used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods entry for raw sea scallops (FDC ID 175177). Nutrient values reflect a grilled preparation without breading, adjusted to remove breading contribution; protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with plain grilled scallop composition. Garlic-butter-soy sauce condiment contribution is minor and reflected in sodium and fat estimates. This is an adapted match; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled scallops. Scallops are a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate shellfish (approximately 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g). GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other lean shellfish and seafood items (e.g., shrimp, fish) which consistently show GI values in the range 0–40 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature, reflecting minimal glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 180 g serving = (25 × 4.32 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.1, confirming negligible glycemic load. gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Grilled scallops prepared with soy sauce and garlic butter contribute approximately 392 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium seasoning or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index estimated at 25, grilled scallops have negligible glycemic impact and are a suitable high-protein, low-GI seafood choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled scallops provide lean protein (20.5 g per 100 g) with low saturated fat (0.6 g) and a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio; they are a heart-healthy seafood option, though sodium from seasoning should be monitored in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled scallops (烤扇贝) are a popular Chinese seafood dish served at seafood restaurants in Singapore, typically presented as 6 pieces per serving on the half-shell. The scallops are cooked over a charcoal grill or BBQ flame and topped with a savoury mixture of minced garlic, butter, soy sauce, and spring onion, which caramelises during grilling to produce a rich, umami-laden flavour. The texture of properly grilled scallops is tender and slightly firm, with a sweet, briny taste characteristic of fresh bivalve molluscs. Nutritionally, grilled scallops are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 20.5 g per 100 g), low in fat (2.4 g per 100 g), and very low in carbohydrates (2.4 g per 100 g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. They are rich in vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and potassium. The glycemic index is estimated at 25 (low), with a negligible glycemic load per serving, making them appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate due to soy sauce seasoning and should be considered by individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Scallops are classified as molluscs and are a common allergen. This dish is widely available at Chinese seafood BBQ restaurants and hawker-style seafood markets in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled scallops?
Are scallops good for heart health?
How much sodium in BBQ scallops?
What is the GI of scallops?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked scallop data (plain, without breading), cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods raw scallop entry. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for grilled scallops with garlic-butter-soy sauce preparation was found. GI is an analogous estimate based on shellfish category data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to adapted nutrient source and estimated GI.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this record. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source for cooked scallops. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — scallops contain no industrially produced trans fats. Sugar (sugar_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked scallops. Dietary fibre (dietary_fibre_g) is 0.0; naturally zero as scallops are an animal-derived shellfish product with no plant fibre. Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked scallops. The cuisine tag 'Crab' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tag error and has not been carried forward; this record is for scallops only. Serving size of 180 g is estimated based on 6 pieces at approximately 30 g per scallop (medium sea scallop). Sodium value of 392 mg per 100 g reflects grilled preparation with soy sauce and garlic butter seasoning; plain raw scallops have approximately 392 mg sodium per 100 g per USDA data, which is consistent with the seasoned preparation estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (25) × available carbohydrate per serving (4.32 g from 2.4 g/100g × 180 g) / 100 = 1.1.

Added all mandatory multilingual names for Chinese cuisine (Singaporean context). Populated all ontology anchors with real codes and closest_parent match types. Documented all zero and -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Calculated glycemic load per serving. Set GI to 25 (low) with analogous estimate documentation.
